one_line_takeaway: Feeding a conservative SAST scanner's findings into an LLM's prompt lets the LLM find substantially more missed vulnerabilities (F1 ~39% -> ~77% on a tiny benchmark), while the paper's own RAG knowledge-retrieval layer turns out to hurt more than it helps.
key_finding: Injecting the SAST scanner's results into the LLM prompt sharply improves the LLM's ability to find NEW vulnerabilities beyond the scanner: raw-LSAST (TC+SAST results, no RAG) reached 68.89% TP-Rate, 62% accuracy, 86.11% precision, and 76.54% F1, versus the raw LLM at 17.91% TP-Rate, 24% accuracy, 63.16% precision, 38.71% F1 (Table III). Counterintuitively, adding the RAG knowledge-retrieval layer HURT performance: Combined-LSAST dropped to 35.71% TP-Rate / 49.18% F1, because most retrieved HackerOne reports were unrelated to the code's actual CWEs and distracted the model. Among RAG-only variants (Table I) the authors' Abstraction and Combined approaches beat the prior-work Functionality approach (Combined F1 38.71% vs Abstraction 32.26% vs Functionality 18.18%).
core_contribution: The paper introduces LSAST (LLM-supported Static Application Security Testing), which augments an LLM code-vulnerability scanner by (1) running a conservative open-source SAST scanner (Bearer) first and injecting its formatted findings (CWE IDs + line ranges) into the LLM prompt, instructing the LLM to find only ADDITIONAL vulnerabilities beyond those already flagged; (2) a RAG knowledge-retrieval layer that indexes 873 recent HackerOne bug-bounty reports and retrieves relevant ones via two similarity strategies -- a code-'functionality' summary search (adopted from prior work LLM4Vuln) and the authors' own novel code-'abstraction'/minification search; and (3) using a locally hostable open-source model (Llama-3-70B) to avoid sending proprietary code to third-party LLM providers. The paper's claimed original contributions are the SAST-into-prompt synergy, the abstraction-based retrieval, and the finding that poorly-targeted RAG hurts rather than helps.
threat_model: This is a defensive tooling paper for source-code vulnerability detection (SAST). The security concerns addressed are: (a) undetected exploitable vulnerabilities in source code (e.g., OS command injection CWE-78, SQL injection CWE-943, XXE CWE-611) that conventional pattern-matching SAST scanners miss; (b) LLMs' static training cutoff excluding newly disclosed vulnerabilities; and (c) privacy/IP-leakage and training-data-extraction risk from transmitting proprietary code to third-party LLM API providers, which the paper mitigates by hosting an open-source LLM locally. It is not an attack paper; the LLM is the defender's tool.
limitations: Evaluation is very small and methodologically fragile. (1) Scale: only 10 files across 4 benchmark apps plus a single real-world package. (2) The reported FP-Rate is 100% in every single row of every table -- an artifact the authors admit arises because every test file contains at least one vulnerability, so a 'no-vulnerability' negative never occurs; this makes precision/accuracy hard to interpret and the metric near-meaningless as reported. (3) The metric DEFINITIONS in the paper are muddled/mislabeled -- e.g., 'False Positive Rate' is defined as 'the proportion of vulnerabilities that were overlooked' (which is a false-negative notion), and 'True Positive Rate' is defined as precision-like ('proportion of reported vulnerabilities that were actual'), so the numbers should be read with caution. (4) In Stage 1, ground truth is Bearer's own output, making the task circular (measuring whether the LLM reproduces the very scanner it is meant to augment). (5) Stage-2 TP/FP/FN labels are assigned by the authors themselves, with no independent adjudication or inter-rater check. (6) The flagship RAG contribution is shown to be counterproductive, and the authors attribute this partly to low-quality code extracted from HackerOne's mixed prose+code reports. (7) The pyDust real-world result is internally inconsistent: the text lists 2 High + 1 Medium + 3 Low (=6) yet calls it '5 true positives' plus 2 false positives. (8) Only one model (Llama-3-70B) is tested; the abstract's promise to 'compare with a state-of-the-art LLM' appears to be satisfied only by the raw-vs-LSAST comparison of that same Llama-3-70B, with no comparison against a stronger closed model.

datasets_used: 873 HackerOne bug-bounty vulnerability reports (past year, with code) used as the RAG knowledge base
pyDust Python package (single real-world test target)
Evaluation corpus: 10 files across the 4 benchmark apps, spanning 15+ vulnerability types and languages (Java, JavaScript, TypeScript, PHP)
quantitative_results: Table III (new vulns beyond Bearer, Llama-3-70B): raw LLM TP-Rate 17.91%, accuracy 24%, precision 63.16%, F1 38.71%
Table III: raw-LSAST (TC + SAST results, no RAG) TP-Rate 68.89%, accuracy 62%, precision 86.11%, F1 76.54% -- best configuration
Table III: Combined-LSAST (RAG + SAST results) TP-Rate 35.71%, accuracy 32.61%, precision 78.95%, F1 49.18% -- RAG degraded performance vs raw-LSAST
Table I (RAG-only, ground truth = Bearer findings): Combined TP-Rate 28.57% / F1 38.71%, Abstraction 23.81% / F1 32.26%, Functionality 14.29% / F1 18.18%

Table II (similarity-search relevance): Abstraction TP-Rate 33.33% / precision 16.67% / F1 22.22% vs Functionality 16.67% / precision 10.53% / F1 12.90%; ~67% of abstraction-retrieved reports were unrelated to Bearer's CWEs
FP-Rate reported as 100% in every row of every table (evaluation artifact: no vulnerability-free negatives exist)
Real-world pyDust scan: 5 claimed true positives (text lists 2 High + 1 Medium + 3 Low-risk) missed by conventional scanners, plus 2 false positives
baselines_compared: Raw / Own-Knowledge LLM (Llama-3-70B with target code only, no SAST results, no RAG)
Functionality-similarity RAG approach (baseline adopted from prior work LLM4Vuln [23])
Bearer conservative SAST scanner (used both as the augmentation source and, in Stage 1, as ground truth)
Internal ablations: Abstraction, Combined, raw-LSAST, Combined-LSAST configurations
